metadata.career_relevance: Merge K Sorted Lists is a top Flipkart/Amazon hard question. It combines linked lists with heaps (priority queues). Sort Linked List demonstrates why merge sort is preferred over quicksort for linked lists — there's no O(1) random access so quicksort's partition is expensive.

hook.real_world_problem:
```
Flipkart: merge K sorted product price feeds from K suppliers
Each feed is a sorted linked list of prices
Need: one merged sorted list in O(N log K) time

Naive: merge pairwise → O(NK) time
Heap approach:
  Push head of each list into min-heap
  Extract min → push its next
  O(N log K) — N total nodes, log K per heap op
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Merge two sorted lists, merge K with heap, sort linked list with merge sort.

visual_aid.description: Merge K sorted lists with min-heap. 3 lists: [1,4,7], [2,5,8], [3,6,9]. Heap starts with heads: {1,2,3}. Extract 1(min), push 4 → heap {2,3,4}. Extract 2, push 5 → {3,4,5}. Continues producing 1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9. Total: N=9 extractions, each O(log K=log3). Total O(N log K).
visual_aid.alt_text: Merge K sorted lists with min-heap

worked_example.scenario: Sort Linked List (LeetCode #148) — merge sort on LL.
worked_example.problem_statement: Sort linked list in O(n log n) time, O(log n) space.

step_by_step_solution.action: Find middle (fast=head.next to avoid equal-length split loop)
step_by_step_solution.details: fast starts at head.next so slow stops at the END of first half. slow.next is the start of second half. Set slow.next=null to split.

things_to_notice: fast=head.next (not head): ensures proper split for even-length lists
slow.next=null: physically splits the list into two
Merge sort on LL: O(n log n) time, O(log n) space (recursion depth)
Bottom-up merge sort: O(1) space — avoid recursion overhead

industry_examples.use_case: Merge K sorted price feeds from K suppliers: heap-based O(N log K) vs naive O(NK).

applications.interview_relevance: Merge K Sorted Lists is the canonical heap+LL problem. 'Sort LL in O(n log n) O(1) space' is a follow-up that requires bottom-up merge sort.
applications.real_world_workflow: Merge K lists: PriorityQueue<ListNode> with custom comparator. Push all heads, extract min, push extracted.next. Continue until heap empty.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using fast=head instead of fast=head.next for the split. With fast=head, for a 2-node list [1,2]: slow=2, mid=null — impossible to split.
common_gaps.remediation: Start fast at head.next: for [1,2], slow=1 (stops immediately since fast.next=null), mid=2. For [1,2,3,4]: slow=2, mid=3. Correct split every time.

key_takeaways: Merge two sorted: dummy + curr + while(l1&&l2) + attach remainder
Merge K: min-heap PriorityQueue, push heads, extract-push loop
Sort LL: merge sort — find middle (fast=head.next), split, sort halves, merge
Why merge sort not quicksort on LL: no O(1) random access for pivot swap
Split trick: fast=head.next ensures correct split for even-length lists

self_check_questions.question: Why is merge sort preferred over quicksort for linked lists?
self_check_questions.answer: Merge sort is preferred because: (1) Finding the middle is O(n/2) with fast/slow pointers — acceptable overhead. (2) The merge step naturally works on linked lists without extra space — just pointer rewiring. Quicksort's problem: (1) partitioning requires swapping arbitrary elements — on a linked list this means O(n) traversal for each pivot partition. (2) There's no O(1) random access to swap elements at positions i and j. So quicksort on LL degrades to O(n²) average. Merge sort maintains O(n log n) with O(log n) recursion stack space (or O(1) with bottom-up merge sort).

reflection.next_topic_preview: Module 32 complete! Next: M33 DSA5 — Stacks & Queues (monotonic stack, sliding window max, implement queue with stack).
